title: AWS Deployment Strategy for WorkMate
content:
Backend (FastAPI) — 3 Options
Option A: EC2 Instance
• Full control over the server — install anything, SSH in, run background tasks natively
• Good for our use case since we have long-running background tasks (Notion sync can take minutes with rate limits)
• t3.micro is free tier eligible (750 hrs/month for 12 months)
• We'd need to manage our own deployment (Docker, systemd, or PM2), SSL (via Nginx + Let's Encrypt), and updates
• Cost: $0 (free tier) or ~$8/month after
Option B: AWS App Runner
• Fully managed — deploy from a Docker image or GitHub repo, auto-scales, handles HTTPS
• No server management, no SSH, no Nginx config
• Supports environment variables for secrets
• Concern: background tasks (Notion ingestion) may timeout — App Runner is designed for request/response, not long-running processes
• Cost: ~$5-10/month (scales to zero when idle)
Option C: AWS Lambda + API Gateway (Serverless)
• Each API endpoint becomes a Lambda function, triggered by API Gateway
• Pay per request — essentially free at our traffic volume
• Major concern: cold starts add 1-3 seconds to first request, which hurts the chat experience. SSE streaming is also problematic on Lambda since it has a response
timeout
• ChromaDB doesn't work well in Lambda (needs persistent disk)
• Would require significant code refactoring (FastAPI → Mangum adapter, or rewrite as individual handlers)
• Cost: ~$0-2/month at capstone demo traffic
Database (PostgreSQL) — 2 Options
Option A: AWS RDS (PostgreSQL)
• Managed PostgreSQL — automatic backups, patching, replication
• Our SQLAlchemy ORM already supports Postgres — migration is a one-line DATABASE_URL change
• db.t3.micro is free tier eligible
• Cost: $0 (free tier) or ~$13/month after
Option B: PostgreSQL on IBM LinuxONE
• Host PostgreSQL on IBM LinuxONE alongside ChromaDB
• Keeps both databases on the same infrastructure
• Satisfies the professor's IBM requirement more substantially
• We'd manage PostgreSQL ourselves (install, backup, configure)
• Cost: $0 (IBM Community Cloud is free for academic use)
Vector Database (ChromaDB)
IBM LinuxONE
• Already committed to this in our IBM LinuxONE summary for Prof. Banafa
• ChromaDB runs as a persistent service on LinuxONE
• The backend connects to it over the network instead of local disk
• Cost: $0 (IBM Community Cloud)
Frontend (React) — 2 Options
Option A: Cloudflare Pages
• From the 4/8 meeting: "Cloudflare is straightforward"
• Free tier covers our needs (unlimited bandwidth, 500 builds/month)
• Auto-deploys from GitHub, custom domain, HTTPS included
• Store the deployed URL as a secret in AWS for the backend's FRONTEND_URL
• Cost: $0
Option B: AWS S3 + CloudFront
• Upload dist/ to S3, serve via CloudFront CDN
• More AWS-native if we want everything in one ecosystem
• Slightly more setup than Cloudflare
• Cost: ~$1/month
Secrets Management
AWS Secrets Manager
• Stores API keys, OAuth secrets, JWT secret, Fernet encryption key
• Integrates with EC2/App Runner — secrets injected as environment variables
• Cost: ~$0.40/secret/month (~$3/month for our ~7 secrets)
• Alternative: just use EC2 environment variables or a .env file on the server (free but less secure)

title: Week 12/13 Ruby’s Updates
content:
### The Database Properties Question
Even though properties are now in chunk text and searchable, the RAG pipeline only retrieves top-k chunks (15 from hybrid retrieval → 10 after reranking). It never sees the full corpus. So: 
• “What is the status of Task X?” - Works. Retrieval finds the specific row, properties are in the chunk.
• “what tasks are assigned to Alice?” - Partially works. Retrieves a few matching rows, but may miss some if there are man.
• “How many research notes tags are there?” - Cannot work. Requires scanning ALL documents, counting/aggregating. The LLM only sees ~10 chunks and would either hallucinate a number or say “I cannot find this”. 
Aggregation queries (count, sum, list-all, compare-across-many) are outside RAG’s capability. Solving this would require a separate query path - e.g., a metadata index with SQL-like queries, or a pre-computer summary layer. This is a future consideration, not something to address in the current pipeline improvements.

title: Findings & Updates
content:
• Last 6 messages are passed to LLM at generation time. They are for conversation context so that Gemini understands the follow-up questions based on the previous messages. 
• Killing /api/chat/ask end point —> routers/chat.py and schemas/chat.py
• chroma_db in GitHub
• Fixed issue in LLL-re-ranking.
• Updated routers/conversation.py and schemas/conversation.py 
• added debug info so that when debug is true, we can see. debug default is false
• unfiltered chunks - everything retrieved from chromadb + sibling expansion
• filtered chunks - what LLM re-ranking kept
• final chunks. - what was actually sent to LLM for generation (after 3 chunks / 4000 characters cap)
• 2026-03-21 - 12:47AM 
To get fresh data , run the Notion fetcher manually first: 
From the project root: 
python src/Notion/notion_fetcher/main.py or
python src/Notion/scripts.py
That saves fresh data to src/data/notion_data.json, then you run the ingestor:
cd src/backend/transform
python notion_ingestory.py
The direct Notion API integration is still listed as "In Progress" in CLAUDE.md — there's no endpoint that pulls + ingests in one step yet.
Updates
Chunking Improvements
Switched Notion ingestion to RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter only
• Removed MarkdownHeaderTextSplitter — it produced too many tiny chunks (50–499 chars) that hurt retrieval quality
• Pure recursive chunking gives more uniform, context-rich chunks
Increased chunk size for Notion: 500 → 1000, overlap 100 → 200
• Halved chunk count (269 → 135 chunks)
• Each chunk now carries more context, reducing cases where a retrieved chunk is too short to be useful
Retrieval Improvements
BM25 hybrid search with RRF (Reciprocal Rank Fusion)
• Added bm25s keyword index alongside ChromaDB vector search
• Catches exact-match queries that semantic/vector search misses (e.g. searching by a specific page name or term)
• BM25 indexes page title + chunk text so title keyword matches score correctly
• Both sources return top 15, merged via RRF (k=60) → final top 15 candidates
Sibling expansion
• If a retrieved chunk is < 100 chars (too short to be useful), fetches up to 5 adjacent chunks from the same parent document
The problem it solves: Sometimes a chunk lands in ChromaDB that's very short — e.g. a heading like "## Deadlines" or a single bullet. That chunk might
score well in retrieval because it matches the query, but it carries almost no useful information on its own.
What sibling expansion does: It looks at the parent_id metadata of that short chunk. All chunks that were split from the same source page share the same
parent_id. So it fetches up to 5 other chunks from that same page and adds them to the candidate pool.
parent_ids_to_expand = {
chunk.get("parent_id")
for chunk in all_chunks
if len(chunk["text"].strip()) < 100 and chunk.get("parent_id")
}
for parent_id in parent_ids_to_expand:
sibling_results = hybrid.chroma.get_by_parent(parent_id, limit=5)
Important clarification: "adjacent" was slightly imprecise on my part. It fetches chunks by parent_id match — not strictly the chunks immediately
before/after in sequence. It's the nearest chunks from the same source page, ordered by whatever ChromaDB returns (likely chunk_index order). So it's
same-page siblings, not necessarily positionally adjacent.
Then what: The sibling chunks are added to all_chunks and go through reranking alongside everything else. The reranker then decides which of them are
actually relevant to the query — so sibling expansion just widens the candidate pool, it doesn't blindly include everything.
Reranking
Replaced LLM-based chunk filtering with VoyageAI reranker (rerank-2)
• Old approach: sent chunks to Gemini and asked it to pick relevant ones — slow (800–1500ms), inconsistent
• New approach: VoyageAI cross-encoder scores every chunk against the query directly — ~200ms, more accurate
• Page title + section prepended to each document before scoring for richer signal
• Filters by relevance threshold (0.3), returns top 10
• Bumped reranker top_k from 3 → 10
Graceful degradation if VOYAGE_API_KEY is absent
• Logs a warning, falls back to top-k RRF results unranked — app stays functional
Generation Improvements
Parent-child metadata enrichment
• Child chunks retain parent document context via parent_title metadata
• Child page summaries deliberately not appended to parent content — prevents cross-contamination where unrelated child pages pollute parent retrieval
Conversation history in generation
• Last 6 messages passed to Gemini alongside retrieved chunks
• Allows follow-up questions to resolve references ("what about the frontend?" after asking about auth)
• send_message uses [-6:] (user msg not yet committed); stream_message uses [-7:-1] (user msg already committed, excludes it)
Context cap increased: 4000 → 5000 chars
• More retrieved content can reach Gemini without truncation
BM25 vs Sparse Embedding: 
● Conceptually similar
Both are built on the idea that the words in a document matter literally — if the query says "deadline" and the document contains "deadline", that's a
strong signal. Neither tries to understand meaning; they reward term overlap
This is the opposite of dense embeddings, which might match "deadline" to "due date" or "submission cutoff" because they encode semantic meaning into
geometry. BM25 and sparse embeddings would miss that connection entirely unless those exact words appear.
if sparse embedding knows the due date means deadline, then what is the difference between sparse embedding vs vector embedding?
The difference is how they encode meaning and what the vector dimensions represent.
Sparse embedding
Dimensions map to vocabulary tokens — the vector has one slot per word in the vocabulary (~30,000–50,000 slots).
"deadline" → [0, 0, ..., 0.8,  0, ..., 0.3,  0, ...]
^              ^
"deadline"     "due date"
• Most values are 0
• Non-zero values = tokens the model thinks are relevant to this text
• You can read what it activated — "oh, it fired on deadline, due date, submission"
• Matching is still token-based: compare which vocabulary slots are non-zero between query and document
Dense (vector) embedding
Dimensions are learned abstract features — no human-readable meaning.
"deadline" → [0.23, -0.87, 0.41, 0.09, -0.31, ...]  (768 values, all non-zero)
• Every dimension has a value
• No dimension means anything on its own — meaning lives in the geometry (distance/angle between vectors)
• "deadline" and "due date" land close together in 768-dimensional space because they were trained to
The remaining gap
Sparse embeddings still think in tokens. If a concept has no close vocabulary token — abstract ideas, domain jargon, cross-lingual meaning — sparse
embeddings struggle. Dense embeddings encode the concept into geometry regardless of what words were used, which is why they handle paraphrase, synonyms,
and cross-language retrieval better.
That's why production RAG systems (including what we're building toward) often use all three — BM25 + sparse + dense — merged via RRF. Each catches what
the others miss.

title: Performance Testing
content:
# Core Types of Performance Testing
Performance testing includes several specialized categories: 
• Load Testing: Evaluates system behavior under expected normal and peak user loads to identify bottlenecks.
• Stress Testing: Pushes the system beyond its normal limits to find the breaking point and evaluate how it recovers from failure.
• Endurance (Soak) Testing: Monitors the system under a steady load for a prolonged period (hours or days) to detect issues like memory leaks or performance degradation.
• Spike Testing: Checks how the system handles sudden, sharp increases in user traffic.
• Scalability Testing: Determines the system's ability to "scale up" (e.g., adding CPU/RAM) or "scale out" (adding more servers) as demand grows.
• Volume (Flood) Testing: Focuses on the system's performance when processing massive amounts of data in a database.
# Key Metrics to Monitor
To assess performance, track specific Key Performance Indicators (KPIs): 
• Response Time: How long it takes for the system to respond to a user request.
• Throughput: The number of requests or transactions a system processes per second.
• Resource Utilization: Percentage of CPU, memory (RAM), disk I/O, and network bandwidth used.
• Error Rate: The percentage of failed requests compared to the total number of requests.
• Latency: The delay before data transfer begins following an instruction.
# Performance Testing Process
1. Identify Environment & Tools: Document the hardware, software, and network configurations for both test and production environments.
1. Define Acceptance Criteria: Establish specific goals for response times, throughput, and resource use.
1. Plan & Design Tests: Create realistic test scenarios based on anticipated user behavior.
1. Configure Environment: Prepare the testing environment to mirror production as closely as possible.
1. Execute Tests: Run scripts to simulate load and monitor performance in real-time.
1. Analyze & Retest: Identify bottlenecks, optimize code or infrastructure, and re-run tests until goals are met.
# Popular Tools
• Apache JMeter: An open-source tool for load testing and measuring performance on various services.
• OpenText LoadRunner: A comprehensive commercial tool that simulates thousands of users for large-scale enterprise testing.
• Gatling: A high-performance, code-based load testing tool designed for DevOps and CI/CD pipelines.
• k6: A modern, developer-centric tool for testing APIs and microservices using JavaScript.
• BlazeMeter: A cloud-based platform that scales open-source tests (like JMeter) to millions of users.

title: Self-Updating Knowledge Base
content:
# Research
• Handling updates in Chroma
• Option 1: Upserts
• Update collection.add() to collection.upsert() so new documents (new ID) are inserted and changed documents (existing ID) are updated 
• Metadata and ID need to be consistently generated/extracted when 
• Inconsistent IDs can result in duplicate entries and bloat
• Metadata (timestamps) need to also be updated upon upserts
• Option 2: Wipe + Insert
• Before processing new/updated Notion docs, use page_id to delete all documents of that page from Chroma
• Then process incoming data, chunk, embed, and insert
• Handles deleted data well: If incoming page_id is flagged as a delete action rather than update, then documents with matching page_id are deleted, and the processing step is skipped
• Allows for batch inserts, improving performance significantly
• Single upserts < batch inserts
• Chroma instances on IBM need to be replicated, durable, and available
• Deleting data before inserting is risky!
• Detecting updates from Notion
• Option 1: Notion API
• API calls using last_edited_time and created_time to retrieve new and updated documents
• Using a cron job to make these API calls at set intervals, checking for last_edited_time > (now - 1 hour), or whenever the last sync was
• Pro: concrete and consistent, easily programmable
• Con: Risk of hitting rate limits, decreased performance to process
• Option 2: Chroma Sync
• Chroma Cloud feature that handles the ingesting data and keeping Chroma synced with the data source
• Pros: Takes care of the ETL process completely, keeps collections synced and supports dense and sparse embeddings
• Cons: 
• Only pulls data from S3 buckets, Github repos, and web
• Use of S3 would require scraping Notion, storing whole documents in S3, then storing embeddings in Chroma
• Part of Chroma Cloud - paid service
• Chroma Sync
• Potentially requires overhaul of ETL pipeline
• Chroma Sync handles chunking, embedding, and upserts: would make existing ETL scripts redundant
# Implementation Plan
### Overview
• Decoupled Queue on AWS
• Notion updates detection via AWS Lambda
• Triggered by EventBridge every 5 minutes.
• Asks Notion for a list of changed page_ids. It does not download the text or embed anything.
• /databases/{database_id}/query endpoint, include last_edited_time: {"after": "last-time-Lambda-ran"} filter to get page_ids
• Or /search for non-database documents, sorting by last_edited_time , descending
• It takes those IDs and pushes them into an Amazon Simple Queue Service queue.
• SQS acts as a buffer and holds changes safely
• New messages in SQS trigger another Lambda worker that:
• Extracts documents from Notion based on the above page_ids 
• Chunks using existing chunking logic
• Push to Chroma using wipe + insert method
• Pros:  Infinitely scalable, resilient, fast (parallel processing with multiple Lambda workers to handle ETL)
• Cons: IAM between Lambda and SQS to ensure they can communicate
• Note: Hosting backend on AWS and Chroma on IBM will incur latency issues and requires strong security
• Pushing from AWS to IBM will take some time (batch inserts on Chroma > single upserts)
• IBM Chroma instance will need an API key for Lambda to use; no hardcoding IBM URLs
### Cost
AWS Service | Monthly Usage Estimate | Cost | Justification
EventBridge (Cron) | 2,880 triggers (every 15 min) | $0.00 | $1.00 per million events.
Lambda (Detector) | 2,880 invocations (~2 seconds each) | $0.00 | The perpetual free tier gives 1 Million requests and 400,000 GB-seconds free forever. 
SQS (Queue) | 3,000 messages | $0.00 | The perpetual free tier gives 1 Million SQS requests per month.
Lambda (Worker) | 3,000 invocations (~10 seconds each) | $0.00 | Using roughly 15,000 GB-seconds of compute, safely inside the 400,000 free tier limit.
Data Transfer Out | ~50 MB of text chunks sent to IBM | $0.00 | The first 100 GB of internet data transfer out per month is free.
• Use SSM, not Secrets Manager: Store Notion API Key and IBM Chroma URL. AWS Secrets Manager costs $0.40 per secret per month. Use AWS Systems Manager (SSM) Parameter Store. Standard parameters are 100% free.
• Beware the Infinite Retry Loop: If Worker Lambda fails (e.g., IBM Cloud goes down and rejects Chroma connection), SQS will automatically put the message back in the queue and try again. If it fails forever, AWS will keep firing Lambdas and eventually incur costs. Configure a Dead Letter Queue (DLQ). This tells SQS to move failed messages to DLQ and stop trying after X number of failures.
• Batching limits: Ensure Worker Lambda pulls batches of messages (e.g., 10 at a time) from SQS rather than firing a separate Lambda container for every single Notion page. This reduces Lambda invocations by 90%.

uv run src/Notion/scripts.py

=== Fetching Pages ===

```

title: User Data Privacy & Access Control
content:
What does "User Data Privacy & Access Control" mean in WorkMate?
• 
Key concepts to research (Role-based access control, metadata filtering, secure API endpoints)
## Implementation Steps
• Step 1: Database Model Updates
• Step 2: "Connect Notion" API Endpoints
• Step 3: User-Specific Data Ingestion
• Step 4: Secure the RAG Retrieval Pipeline
## Current Project Context
WorkMate currently has user authentication (via Google OAuth and JWTs) and correctly isolates chat histories per user (e.g., Conversation.user_id == current_user.id).
The Privacy Leak: When a user asks a question, the backend queries the vector database. This means any logged-in user can query and retrieve information from the global
notion_data.json ingestion, regardless of who originally owned or fetched that Notion data.
## Key Concepts to Research
1. Vector DB Metadata Filtering: How to use ChromaDB's where clause to restrict searches to specific metadata criteria (e.g., where={"user_id": user_id}).
1. Notion OAuth / Integration Tokens: How to securely allow individual users to link their Notion accounts (OAuth flows) vs relying on a single global NOTION_TOKEN.
1. Multi-tenant RAG Architecture: Storing data for multiple users in a single vector database collection safely by tagging every chunk of data with an owner ID or workspace ID.
1. Asynchronous ETL for Users: Triggering the Notion fetcher and embedder as a background task via FastAPI when a user connects their Notion account.

title: Tune chunk size and overlap
content:
WorkMate's old chunking defaults (chunk_size=1000, chunk_overlap=200) created broad chunks that mixed multiple topics, hurting retrieval precision.
What we did: Tested 7 configurations (sizes 300/500/750/1000 × overlaps 10%/20%) across 29 Notion docs with 6 test queries.
Winner → Config B (chunk_size=500, chunk_overlap=100)
 | OLD | NEW | Change
Chunks | 159 | 240 | +51%
Avg length | 470 chars | 319 chars | -32%
### Chunks: 159 → 240 (+51%)
With the new setting, they become 240 pieces. That's 51% more chunks — meaning each chunk is more focused on a single topic instead of mixing multiple topics together.
Why +51% is good: More chunks = when a user asks a question, the search finds a chunk that's specifically about that topic, not a big chunk that's half-relevant and half-noise.
### Avg length: 470 → 319 chars (-32%)
Each chunk is now 32% shorter on average. Instead of ~470 characters per chunk (which can cover 3–4 different ideas), each chunk is now ~319 characters (usually covering 1–2 ideas).
Why -32% is good: Shorter chunks mean less irrelevant text gets sent to the Gemini LLM. The LLM sees only what's relevant to the question, so it gives more precise answers.
### What we did
1. Loads your 29 Notion docs from src/data/notion_data.json
1. Defines 6 test queries (factoid, broad, list, analytical) to evaluate retrieval
1. Defines 7 configs to test — sizes 300/500/750/1000 × overlaps 10%/20%
### Why need 6 test queries
Different chunk sizes perform differently depending on the type of question being asked. Here's why each query type matters
Query | Type | What it tests
"Who is responsible for deployment?" | Factoid | Can it find one specific fact? → Smaller chunks win
"What is the purpose of WorkMate?" | Broad | Can it find a general overview? → Larger chunks win
"What happened during the last incident?" | Event | Can it isolate a specific event? → Smaller chunks win
"What are the sprint tasks for the backend?" | List | Can it retrieve a full list without cutting it off? → Medium chunks win
"How does the CI/CD pipeline work?" | Analytical | Can it piece together a multi-step process? → Larger chunks win
"What are the main features?" | Summary | Can it pull high-level info? → Larger chunks win
The point: If you only tested with one query type (e.g., factoid), you might pick a chunk size that's great for quick lookups but terrible for analytical questions. By testing across all types, you find the best overall balance — which is why 500/100 won: it's small enough for factoids but still large enough for analytical queries

title: Midterm Report Guidelines Info
content:
### Midterm Report Guidelines
(Use attached word doc)
1. Minimum 10 pages (including cover page and references).
1. Submit using MS Word only (not PPT).
1. Focus on your project and team’s work, not on general technologies (e.g., Java, JS, SQL, Android Studio).
1. Include screenshots of your web and mobile pages.
1. All charts, diagrams, and figures must be labeled and explained.
1. Include a cover page and a references page.
1. List any sources you used in the references section.
1. Clearly include team members’ names and ID numbers.

title: LLM Hallucination Fix Walkthrough
content:
# LLM Hallucination Fix Walkthrough
## Issue Diagnosis
The user reported that the LLM was hallucinating responses to RAG queries and making up information/fake documents rather than obeying the critical rules defined in
prompts.py.
Through investigation and writing a testing script
test_rag.py, we discovered two root causes:
1. When the user queries something that has zero matching documents in the ChromaDB vector store, the retrieval step returned no documents (chunks=[]).
1. When the LLM received an empty context block (i.e. CONTEXT CHUNKS: ), it would ignore the fallback rule "I cannot find this in your Notion docs" and instead attempt to fulfill the query using its pre-trained knowledge, aggressively hallucinating project ideas and fake chunk citations.
## Implementation Fixes
To resolve this, I made the instruction prompt significantly more robust and explicit in
…/WorkMate/src/backend/llm/prompts.py:
1. Explicit Empty State in Formatter: Modified _format_chunks to explicitly return CONTEXT CHUNKS:\n(None provided) when the chunks list is empty, rather than just omitting the chunks. This provides a tangible string for the LLM to ground against when deciding if context is missing.
1. Strict Grounding Rule: Updated the instructions in get_rag_prompt to strongly command the LLM not to invent facts or guess explicitly if it encounters the (None provided) string or unrelated chunks.
### Code Changed:
```diff
--- src/backend/llm/prompts.py
+++ src/backend/llm/prompts.py
@@ -21,6 +21,9 @@
     Build a clean, deterministic context block with explicit chunk IDs + page titles + section + paragraph.
     Each chunk must contain: chunk_id, page_title, text, (optionally section, paragraph)
     """
+    if not chunks:
+        return "CONTEXT CHUNKS:\n(None provided)"
+
     lines = ["CONTEXT CHUNKS:"]
@@ -64,11 +64,11 @@
     {question}

     INSTRUCTIONS:
-    1) Answer ONLY using the context chunks above.
+    1) Answer ONLY using the information explicitly stated in the CONTEXT CHUNKS above.
     2) If multiple chunks mention the topic, prefer the most specific one.
-    3) If no chunk clearly answers, reply exactly: "I cannot find this in your Notion docs."
+    3) CRITICAL: If the CONTEXT CHUNKS do not contain the answer, or if the CONTEXT CHUNKS say "(None provided)", you MUST reply exactly: "I cannot find this in your Notion docs." DO NOT invent features, ideas, or files.
     4) Output MUST include inline citations for the source of its findings in the exact format: [Document name, section, paragraph] (section and paragraph are optional if not provided).{debug_instruction}

-    Remember: Do not use outside knowledge.
+    Remember: Do not guess. Do not use outside knowledge.
     """.strip()
```
## Validation
I wrote a test script that verified the LLM now reliably outputs Answer: I cannot find this in your Notion docs. \nConfidence: High instead of hallucinating when facing questions about missing concepts (like "What are the project ideas for the application?" when it has 0 retrieved documents).
(Note: During final end-to-end testing with actual documents populated in ChromaDB, the Gemini API hit a 429 RESOURCE_EXHAUSTED free-tier rate limit, but the logical fix successfully addresses the core prompt engineering vulnerability).
## Additional Fixes (Empty Chunks on Valid Queries)
During testing, we discovered that even valid queries were returning
(None provided) to the LLM. Investigating the ChromaDB query step revealed that the vector store was returning 0 results (
```plain text
{'documents': [[]]}
```
) due to a collection deletion bug.
### 1. ChromaManager Conflict Deletion Bug
In
ChromaManager (file:
src/backend/load/chroma_manager.py
), an exception handler was intercepting
```plain text
ValueError: Embedding function conflict
```
. The previous code proactively deleted the entire collection and recreated it using the default 384-dimension embedder instead of the custom 3072-dimension
GoogleEmbedder
.
Fix: Changed ChromaManager.__init__ to silently ignore the conflict, retrieve the existing collection, and manually assign the custom embedding function to self.collection._embedding_function so that dimensions match. Also updated ChromaManager.reset() to explicitly pass embedding_function=self.embedder.
### 2. GoogleEmbedder Rate Limits
Re-ingesting the
notion_data.json exposed a rate limit
```plain text
429 RESOURCE_EXHAUSTED
```
(100 RPM) on the free-tier Gemini API, as
GoogleEmbedder
embedded documents in a fast loop without delays.
Fix: Added a time.sleep(0.65) per document inside GoogleEmbedder.__call__ to stay well under the 100 requests-per-minute quota during ingestion.
After fixing these pipeline flaws and running a fresh ingestion, the LLM now receives valid chunks and answers accurately without hallucinating!
(Note: During final end-to-end testing with actual documents populated in ChromaDB, the Gemini API hit a 429 RESOURCE_EXHAUSTED free-tier rate limit, but the logical fix successfully addresses the core prompt engineering vulnerability).

title: VectorDB Updates
content:
# Requirements and Overview
When changes are made in Notion, they need to be reflected in the vectorDB. These vectorDB updates should:
• Only update modified chunks
• Large modifications may need to be chunked
• Occur automatically at a fixed schedule (frequency can be adjusted, no longer than 1 day, no shorter than 30min)
• Include manual update option with a cooldown
 Key issue is figuring out how to update the vectorDB; we can’t just reset it and re-scrape everything.
# Quick Research
• ChromaDB features update() and upsert() command to handle DB updates
• IDs supplied must correspond to existing rows for update(), otherwise update will be aborted for that row
• If an ID is not found when using upsert(), a new vector is inserted instead
• Note: How do we retrieve the ID for the chunk we want to update?
• Chunk management: Let’s say we have a chunk size of 1000. An existing chunk is updated and more information is added to it, lengthening it by 500.
• Old chunk in Chroma is 1000, while new data in Notion is 1500. How do we handle this update?  Chunk new data and insert, while deleting the old chunk? Split new data into two chunks (1000/500), update old chunk with new 1000-chunk, and insert new 500-chunk?
• If a chunk in Notion is shortened, then it can just be updated directly using update()
• Metadata Needed: Source data ID (parent ID, page/block/db ID), last updated, version #
• IDs help identify which vector to update in chroma
• Last updated used to identify if an update is necessary
• Version # is a nice addition to track number of updates
• Could be helpful in debugging as well
• Change Data Capture (CDC) to detect changes in source material
• Detecting Updates/Deletes
• Full Re-indexing 
• Wiping the DB and re-inserting everything, 
• Inefficient
• May requires downtime
• Incremental Updates 
• Update/upsert specific vectors
• Can cause drift and inaccuracies over time if updates fail or changes are missed
• Requires accurate tracking of data changes
• Hybrid (mix of both)
• Incremental updates with schedules jobs + full re-indexing infrequently (weekly/monthly)
• Good balance of both approaches
• Maintains consistency of data and efficiency with minimal full refresh
• Tough implementation
•

title: Fetching data from Notion
content:
## How Notion Structures Data
```plain text
Notion Workspace
    │
    ├── Page
    │     └── Blocks (paragraphs, headings, lists, etc.)
    │           └── Can have nested blocks (children)
    │
    └── Database
          └── Pages (each row is a page)
                └── Properties (columns: title, status, date, etc.)
                └── Blocks (content inside the row)
```
Everything in Notion is either a Page, a Database, or a Block.
## Key API Endpoints
What You Want | Endpoint | Purpose
Find all pages | POST /search | Discover content in the workspace
Get page info | GET /pages/{page_id} | Get page properties (title, etc.)
Get page content | GET /blocks/{block_id}/children | Get the actual text/content
Query a database | POST /databases/{database_id}/query | Get rows from a database
## The Tricky Part: Blocks
Page content is stored as blocks, and there are many types:
Block Type | What It Is
paragraph | Normal text
heading_1, heading_2, heading_3 | Headings
bulleted_list_item | Bullet point
numbered_list_item | Numbered list
to_do | Checkbox item
toggle | Collapsible section (has children!)
code | Code block
quote | Quote block
table | Table (rows are children)
child_page | Nested page
## Data Flow for Fetching
```plain text
1. Search workspace
        │
        ▼
2. Get list of pages/databases
        │
        ▼
3. For each page:
   └── Fetch blocks (content)
       └── If block has children → fetch those too (recursive)
        │
        ▼
4. Combine into clean text chunks
        │
        ▼
5. Ready for embedding
```
## Challenges
Challenge | Why It Happens | Solution
Nested blocks | Toggles, columns, etc. have children | Recursive fetching
Pagination | API returns max 100 items at a time | Handle next_cursor
Rate limits | 3 requests/second for free tier | Add delays between calls
Rich text complexity | Text can have formatting, links, mentions | Extract plain text only
Empty blocks | Some blocks have no text | Skip them
## Architecture Overview

title: Model Selection
content:
Week 4 - Mistral-7B-Instruct-v0.2
We are building a Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) system to answer questions using content from our Notion workspace. The system is designed to:
• Retrieve relevant documents from Notion
• Generate accurate, context-based answers
• Minimize hallucinations
• Support fast, chat-like interactions
• Stay within budget and capstone timeline constraints
## Research Summary
We evaluated multiple Large Language Model (LLM) options based on:
• Cost vs. answer quality
• Ease of integration with Python + ChromaDB
• Performance in RAG-style “answer from context” tasks
• Latency and deployment simplicity
API / Platform | Cost | Setup | Best For
Hugging Face | Free tier | API token | Open-source experimentation
OpenAI | Paid | API key | Production-grade accuracy
Anthropic Claude | Paid | API key | Long context reasoning
Local Models | Free (hardware cost) | GPU required | Privacy, offline usage
Based on these factors, we focused on three core RAG components:
1. Embedding Model
1. Language Model (LLM)
1. Optional Reranker Model
## Embedding Model Selection
### Selected: sentence-transformers/all-MiniLM-L6-v2
Why MiniLM was chosen:
Criteria | Reason
Model Size | ~80 MB (very lightweight)
Speed | Extremely fast (~0.1s per document)
Accuracy | ~0.785 on STS benchmark
Cost | Free via Hugging Face
Use Case | Proven, widely used in RAG pipelines
Alternatives considered:
• BAAI/bge-small-en-v1.5 (slightly better accuracy but significantly larger)
Decision:
MiniLM provides the best balance of speed, accuracy, and reliability for our capstone timeline.
## Language Model (LLM) Selection
### Selected: mistralai/Mistral-7B-Instruct-v0.2
We selected Mistral-7B-Instruct-v0.2 as our final language model due to its strong instruction-following ability and higher answer quality compared to smaller free models.
Why Mistral was chosen:
Criteria | Reason
Answer Quality | Significantly better reasoning than Flan-T5
Instruction Following | Optimized for instruction-based prompts
RAG Performance | Produces clear, context-aware answers
Open Source | Fully open-source model
Scalability | Easy to upgrade to hosted or local GPU later
Trade-offs:
• Requires more compute than Flan-T5
• Hugging Face free tier may have rate limits
Alternatives considered:
• google/flan-t5-large: Very low hallucination but weaker reasoning
• HuggingFaceH4/zephyr-7b-beta: Good quality, but less stable
• microsoft/phi-2: Requires GPU
Decision:
Mistral-7B-Instruct offers the best balance of accuracy, reasoning, and future scalability, making it ideal for a capstone demo that emphasizes correctness and clarity.
## Reranker Model (Optional – Phase 2)
### Planned: BAAI/bge-reranker-base
• Improves ranking of retrieved documents
• Helps reduce irrelevant context passed to the LLM
• Will be added after baseline system is stable
## System Phases
### Phase 1: Baseline System (Weeks 4–6)
• Embedding: all-MiniLM-L6-v2
• Language Model: Mistral-7B-Instruct-v0.2
• Expected Performance:
• Accuracy: 80–90%
• Latency: 2–3 seconds
• Cost: $0 (free tier)
### Phase 2: Enhanced System (Weeks 7–8)
• Add reranker (BAAI/bge-reranker-base)
• Optional GPU deployment for faster inference
• Expected Performance:
• Accuracy: 90–95%
• Improved retrieval precision
## Why Two Different Models Are Required
• Embedding models convert text into vectors for similarity search
• Language models generate natural language answers
• These tasks require different architectures, so one model cannot reliably do both
## Deployment & Integration Plan
1. Use Hugging Face Inference API
1. Store embeddings in ChromaDB
1. Implement pipeline in Python
1. Monitor:
• Retrieval accuracy
• Answer quality
• Hallucination rate (<5%)
• Response time (<3s)
## Final Model Summary
Component | Selected Model | Reason
Embedding | all-MiniLM-L6-v2 | Fast, accurate, lightweight
Language Model | Mistral-7B-Instruct-v0.2 | Best reasoning & instruction following
Reranker | BAAI/bge-reranker-base (optional) | Improves retrieval accuracy
Week 5 - Gemini 3 Flash
We were originally using some basic tools (Mistral and a simple search tool) to get started. But after looking at the "big players" like Google (Gemini) and OpenAI (ChatGPT), I found a much better way. I’ve decided we should switch to Gemini 3 Flash.
Why Gemini 3 Flash? Think of it like upgrading from a library book to a super-fast digital scanner.
• It’s Free & Fast: We can ask it 1,500 questions a day for $0.
• Huge Memory: It can "read" about 1,500 pages of our Notion notes all at once without getting confused.
• It’s Already Working: I’ve already tested the connection, and it’s talking back to me perfectly!
The Technical "Glue" (Embedding): To make this work, we have to use a specific "translator" (called text-embedding-004).
• Right now, our database (ChromaDB) uses a default translator that works fine for testing.
• But as we get closer to the final project, we should switch to Google’s translator so the "Brain" and the "Search" speak the exact same language. If they don't, the system might get "confused" and throw an error

[Page: AI-Assisted Knowledge & Decision System for Technical Projects (Notion → RAG → Analytics Engine)]
### Project Overview
This project focuses on building an AI-assisted knowledge and decision support system for technical projects by integrating Notion-based documentation, structured analytics, and a Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) pipeline. The system ingests project artifacts such as requirements, design documents, technical notes, and code-related text from Notion and other document formats, transforms them into a unified knowledge base, and enables intelligent querying and analysis.
The primary goal is to help developers and project stakeholders understand, evaluate, and make decisions based on existing project knowledge without manually searching through fragmented documents.
### Project Idea
The system combines traditional data engineering with AI by separating knowledge storage, retrieval, and reasoning. A structured analytics layer processes metadata, document versions, and dependencies, while the AI component uses RAG to generate context-aware, source-grounded responses to technical questions. Beyond Q&A, the system provides decision insights, such as identifying outdated documentation, detecting inconsistencies between requirements and implementation notes, and summarizing system architecture.
### High-level idea
Build an AI system that understands project artifacts (docs, code, requirements, logs) and:
• Answers deep technical questions
• Detects inconsistencies
• Generates documentation
• Provides decision insights
### Core components
### 1. Knowledge Infrastructure (Non-AI Heavy, but Complex)
• Ingest data from:
• Notion
• Markdown / Word docs
• Code repositories
• Build:
• metadata store
• embeddings index
• version tracking
• Implement RAG pipeline with chunking strategies
Tech:
• Vector DB (FAISS / Chroma)
• dbt for transformation
• Structured + unstructured fusion
### 2. AI Component
A. Retrieval-Augmented Generation
• Context-aware Q&A
• Cross-document reasoning
• Source citation & confidence scoring
B. Intelligent Insight Generation
• Detect:
• requirement conflicts
• outdated docs vs code
• missing documentation
• Auto-suggest:
• architecture summaries
• test cases
• risk areas
Models:
• Open-source LLM (API-based, not local)
• Prompt engineering + evaluation framework
[Page: AI-Assisted Learning Platform]
## Overview
An AI-assisted learning platform that revolutionizes how students engage with educational content. The platform combines traditional text-based materials with AI-generated video content, creating a dynamic and interactive learning experience.
Educators post text content to the platform, which is automatically accompanied by AI-generated videos that explain and visualize the material. Each video includes embedded interactive quizzes with diverse question types, all generated by AI to assess student comprehension in real-time.
The platform intelligently adapts to student performance by highlighting relevant content sections for frequently missed questions, providing targeted support where it's needed most. For challenging concepts, the system generates memory devices and mnemonics to aid retention and understanding.
Designed for seamless integration with existing learning management systems like Canvas, this platform enhances rather than replaces current educational workflows, making it easy for institutions to adopt and scale.
[Page: TripStory AI: Automatic Travel Day Story Generator]
This project will develop a web/mobile application that helps travelers turn a full day of activities into a polished blog or social media story. Throughout the day, users log multiple short entries with photos/videos, locations, and comments. At the end of the day, an AI component analyzes the timeline and automatically generates a structured travel blog and platform-specific social media posts. The system focuses on usability, content organization, and AI-assisted writing, providing users with a fast way to share detailed, high-quality travel stories.
The idea is to build a system that turns a full day of travel into shareable content (blog, post, or reels) automatically.
## 1. Refine project concept
Proposed title: “TripStory AI: Automatic Travel Day Story Generator”
Core idea:
• Users log small pieces of their day trip (photos/videos, locations, comments, ratings) throughout the day via a mobile app or web app.
• At the end of the day, the system generates:
• A structured blog article.
• Short social media posts (Instagram/Facebook captions, X posts).
• Optional reel script or bullet outline for a short video.
This is conceptually similar to other AI-driven personalization projects in the PPT, but focused on travel content automation.
## DayTale/TripStory AI: Automatic Travel Day Story Generator
Proposal:
We will develop a web/mobile application that helps travelers turn a full day of activities into a polished blog or social media story. Throughout the day, users log multiple short entries with photos/videos, locations, and comments. At the end of the day, an AI component analyzes the timeline and automatically generates a structured travel blog and platform-specific social media posts. The system focuses on usability, content organization, and AI-assisted writing, providing users with a fast way to share detailed, high-quality travel stories.
## Refine project concept
The idea is to build a system that turns a full day of travel into shareable content (blog, post, or reels) automatically.
During a one‑day trip, the user records their experience in small pieces throughout the day:
• Morning:
The user visits a café and uploads photos or short videos, the café location, a quick review, and a brief comment about the experience.
• Later in the morning:
The user goes to a viewpoint, adds more photos/videos, writes a short description of the place, and possibly a rating or quick notes (e.g., crowd level, best time to visit or parking).
• Lunch:
At a restaurant, the user uploads food photos, the restaurant location, a review, and any tips (price range, must‑try dishes).
• Afternoon:
The user goes for a hike or other activities and continues to add images/videos, location tags, and short comments as usual.
• Evening:
Back at the hotel, the user adds final updates, such as how the day ended, overall impressions, and any final photos.
Over the course of the day, the system collects multiple small content entries (images, videos, locations, reviews, short texts) that together describe the full trip.
At the end of the day, when all content is uploaded, the user asks the system to generate:
• A complete travel blog post.
• A summarized social media post.
• Or a script/structure for short video reels.
The system then uses all the collected information (places visited, images, comments, reviews, tips like “how to get there” and “suggestions for others”) to automatically create a well‑structured, ready‑to‑share story of the entire day. The user can then post this directly to platforms like Facebook, Instagram, X (Twitter), or their personal blog.
## High-level system design
1. Data capture during the trip
• Users create multiple “check-ins” during the day:
• Location (GPS or map selection).
• Media (images/videos).
• Short notes (what, why, feelings, tips).
• Tags (café, viewpoint, restaurant, hike, hotel).
1. Content organization and timeline builder
• Backend groups all check-ins by date and builds a chronological day timeline.
• Each event is categorized (morning café, viewpoint, lunch, activity, evening wrap‑up).
1. AI-based content generation
• Use an LLM (through an API) to transform the structured timeline into:
• A travel blog post (intro → sections for each stop → tips/how-to-go → conclusion).
• Short captions and hashtags for social media.
• Control style (formal blog vs casual Instagram style).
1. Preview, edit, and publish
• Web/app interface to preview the generated blog/post.
• User can edit text and choose which photos to include.
• Export: copy HTML/Markdown for a blog, share text for social media, or download as a document.
## 14-week plan (brief)
proposal → midterm → final
• Weeks 1–2: Requirements, user stories, simple UI wireframes, tech stack choice (e.g., React + Node/Flask, plus an AI API).
• Weeks 3–5: Implement user auth, trip creation, and check-in (location + media + notes) screens.

title: Git
content:
Git Repo to clone : https://github.com/RubyRyn/WorkMate.git
### Team Git Rules
• Clone the repo (no ZIP uploads)
• Never push or work on main,
• Always create a branch for your work either existing one or a new one. 
• Open PRs for review before merging
• Pull before pushing
• Do not commit input/, Output/, or large files
• Use clear commit messages
### Set up
1. Clone the repo
1. If you don’t already, install uv.
1. Run uv sync in the repo’s root director. This will start a venv with uv as the package manager and add the dependencies listed in pyproject.toml
1. To add dependencies, use “uv add [PACKAGE]”
1. DO NOT DO THIS ON MAIN; make a new branch as per the workflow below
1. To remove dependencies, use “uv remove [PACKAGE]”
1. Highly recommend checking in with the team about removing a package that is actively being used
1. Lock dependencies
### Folder Structure
WorkMate/
│
├─ input/         ignored # raw input files (CSV, JSON, etc.)
├─ output/        ignored  # generated files (maps, reports, HTMLs)
├─ src/            # Python scripts & modules
├─ tests/          # test scripts (optional)
├─ docs/           # project documentation
│
├─ .gitignore      # ignored files/folders
├─ README.md       # read me file
├─ main.py         # main executable script
├─ pyproject.toml  # uv configuration
├─ uv.lock         # locked dependencies
### Dependencies
Make sure you create virtual environment not to get dependency conflicts with your other projects. And also We are using uv here. 
Use uv sync after cloning to get the exact versions from uv.lock.
python 3.13.9 , pandas , numpy , matplotlib,  jupyter, json ,requests 
Every time you installed a dependency, make sure you run “uv lock” command. It makes sure every team members use the same package versions. 
### Branch Workflow
• main → always stable, only tested code gets merged here
• feature/* → individual work, one branch per task
• bugfix/* → for urgent fixes if needed
• Testing
Type | Example branch name
Feature | feature/username/add-routing
Feature | feature/username/cluster-analysis
Bugfix | bugfix/username/fix-map-render
Testing | testing/username/test-case
 |

# Intelligent Workforce Routing & Load Balancing System (Less AI Integration)

Design a realistic daily delivery/workforce planning system that:

This is NOT just VRP — it’s a planning + optimization + decision intelligence system, similar to what Amazon / FedEx / DoorDash do internally.
### Core components

or

title: Rutvik’s Project Ideas
content:
# 1. AI-Assisted Learning Platform
## Overview
An AI-assisted learning platform that revolutionizes how students engage with educational content. The platform combines traditional text-based materials with AI-generated video content, creating a dynamic and interactive learning experience.
Educators post text content to the platform, which is automatically accompanied by AI-generated videos that explain and visualize the material. Each video includes embedded interactive quizzes with diverse question types, all generated by AI to assess student comprehension in real-time.
The platform intelligently adapts to student performance by highlighting relevant content sections for frequently missed questions, providing targeted support where it's needed most. For challenging concepts, the system generates memory devices and mnemonics to aid retention and understanding.
Designed for seamless integration with existing learning management systems like Canvas, this platform enhances rather than replaces current educational workflows, making it easy for institutions to adopt and scale.
# 2. Curated Playlist Creation App
### Overview
A music playlist creation application that transforms how users discover and organize their music. By leveraging AI technology, the app creates personalized playlists based on user-specified moods, settings, styles, genres, or any other musical preferences.
Users simply input a prompt describing their desired playlist—whether it's "upbeat songs for a morning workout," "melancholic indie for a rainy day," or "90s nostalgia with deep lyrics"—and the AI analyzes multiple dimensions of songs including lyrics, genres, titles, and deeper thematic meanings to curate the perfect collection.
The platform goes beyond simple genre matching by understanding the emotional resonance and contextual fit of each track. Once generated, playlists are fully customizable, allowing users to add, remove, or reorder songs to match their exact preferences.
An intelligent naming feature automatically generates creative and contextual playlist titles based on the user's prompt and the selected songs, though users can always provide their own custom names. This combines the efficiency of AI-powered curation with the personal touch of human refinement.

## 1. AI-Powered Conversational Project Manager
Core Concept: A "Zero-UI" task management system that replaces traditional forms with a Natural Language Interface. It allows teams to manage complex workflows like Jira or Trello through simple conversation, making project management accessible and significantly faster.
• Key Features:

• 
2. Multi-Database Query Interface
Core Concept: A unified data middleware tool designed to eliminate the friction of switching between different database management systems. It provides a single entry point to query across Relational (SQL) and Non-Relational (NoSQL) environments simultaneously.
• Key Features:
• Unified Query Execution: Write a single SQL-like query that targets MySQL, PostgreSQL, and MongoDB in one go.
• Data Normalization: A central engine that transforms BSON and Row-based results into a consistent, unified JSON format.
• Virtual Joins: The ability to join datasets across different engines (e.g., joining a MySQL Users table with a MongoDB UserProfiles collection).
• Technical Implementation:
• Development of a Query Parser to interpret incoming commands.
• Integration of multiple database drivers into a single backend service.
• Implementation of an aggregation layer to handle result merging and deduplication.
## 3. Smart Expense Categorizer
Core Concept: An ML-powered financial management tool that automates the tedious process of tracking expenses. By combining Computer Vision (OCR) and Classification models, it converts unstructured data from receipts and statements into organized financial intelligence.
• Key Features:
• OCR Processing: High-accuracy extraction of vendor, date, and total amount from physical receipt photos.
• Automated Labeling: A trained classifier that identifies categories (e.g., Utilities, Travel, Dining) based on merchant names and transaction patterns.
• Spending Analytics: A dynamic dashboard providing visualizations of monthly burn rates and category-wise spending.
• Technical Implementation:
• Use of Python-based ML libraries (Scikit-learn/TensorFlow) for transaction classification.
• Integration of OCR engines (like Tesseract or AWS Textract) for image-to-text conversion.
• A relational database to store historical data for trend analysis.
## 4. Music generator/sample finder
Core Concept: A creative technology suite for producers that uses Digital Signal Processing (DSP) and AI to navigate audio libraries and generate musical ideas. It shifts the paradigm from "keyword search" to "acoustic similarity search."
• Key Features:
• Acoustic Fingerprinting: Find samples in a local library based on their sonic characteristics (timbre, frequency, rhythm) rather than filenames.
• Generative MIDI: An AI co-composer that suggests chord progressions or drum patterns based on a target mood or genre.
• Auto-Tagging: Automatically identifies BPM, Key, and Instrument Type for untagged audio files.
• Technical Implementation:
• Implementation of Vector Embeddings for audio similarity comparison.
• Use of Magenta.js or Librosa for audio analysis and MIDI generation.
• Building a high-performance indexing system for fast library searching.

content:
## IDEA 1: TripStory AI: Automatic Travel Day Story Generator
This project will develop a web/mobile application that helps travelers turn a full day of activities into a polished blog or social media story. Throughout the day, users log multiple short entries with photos/videos, locations, and comments. At the end of the day, an AI component analyzes the timeline and automatically generates a structured travel blog and platform-specific social media posts. The system focuses on usability, content organization, and AI-assisted writing, providing users with a fast way to share detailed, high-quality travel stories.
The idea is to build a system that turns a full day of travel into shareable content (blog, post, or reels) automatically.
## 1. Refine project concept
Proposed title: “TripStory AI: Automatic Travel Day Story Generator”
Core idea:
• Users log small pieces of their day trip (photos/videos, locations, comments, ratings) throughout the day via a mobile app or web app.
• At the end of the day, the system generates:
• A structured blog article.
• Short social media posts (Instagram/Facebook captions, X posts).
• Optional reel script or bullet outline for a short video.
This is conceptually similar to other AI-driven personalization projects in the PPT, but focused on travel content automation.
## 2. High-level system design
1. Data capture during the trip
• Users create multiple “check-ins” during the day:
• Location (GPS or map selection).
• Media (images/videos).
• Short notes (what, why, feelings, tips).
• Tags (café, viewpoint, restaurant, hike, hotel).
1. Content organization and timeline builder
• Backend groups all check-ins by date and builds a chronological day timeline.
• Each event is categorized (morning café, viewpoint, lunch, activity, evening wrap‑up).
1. AI-based content generation
• Use an LLM (through an API) to transform the structured timeline into:
• A travel blog post (intro → sections for each stop → tips/how-to-go → conclusion).
• Short captions and hashtags for social media.
• Control style (formal blog vs casual Instagram style).
1. Preview, edit, and publish
• Web/app interface to preview the generated blog/post.
• User can edit text and choose which photos to include.
• Export: copy HTML/Markdown for a blog, share text for social media, or download as a document.
## IDEA 2: SkyScan Road: Drone-Based Road Damage Intelligence System
Goal: Automatically detect road surface problems—potholes, cracks, patches, and faded lane markings—from drone-captured aerial video/images, then generate geo-referenced damage maps for city engineers.
Problem: Manual road inspections remain slow, costly, and inconsistent. Dashcams provide poor angles and limited coverage. Drones offer systematic, repeatable aerial surveys with perfect top-down views.
Solution: Consumer drones fly automated missions over road segments, capturing high-resolution overlapping images that AI converts into centimeter-accurate damage orthomosaics and maintenance priority lists.
## IDEA 3. Satellite-Based Poverty & Infrastructure Mapping
Use deep learning on satellite imagery to estimate poverty levels, housing quality, or road conditions in developing regions, inspired by projects like “Predicting income via satellite images.”
• Data: Public satellite imagery + open socioeconomic datasets.
• Output: Maps and dashboards to support NGOs or governments.

## Example Flow
```plain text
User: "What tasks are assigned to Alice?"

Your System:
1. Parses intent → needs task database
2. Calls Notion API → fetches tasks where assignee = "Alice"
3. Formats context → "Here are Alice's tasks: [task1, task2, ...]"
4. Sends to LLM → "Given this data: {context}, answer: {question}"
5. Returns LLM response to user

```
# Tech Stack
### Frontend
I have no idea with the frontend.
### Backend or Orchestrator
I am expecting something with python for example, FastAPI. Then we can use the Official Notion SDK “notion-client” for python. 
## Phase 1: Indexing (One-Time Setup)
This happens before any user asks anything. Think of it as "preparing your library."
```plain text
┌─────────────┐
│   Notion    │  You have pages, databases, notes, etc.
│   Workspace │
└──────┬──────┘
       │
       ▼
┌─────────────┐
│   Chunking  │  Break content into smaller pieces
└──────┬──────┘
       │
       ▼
┌─────────────┐
│  Embedding  │  Convert each chunk to a vector (numbers)
└──────┬──────┘
       │
       ▼
┌─────────────┐
│  Vector DB  │  Store all vectors, ready for searching
└─────────────┘

```
When does this run?
• Once at the beginning
• Again whenever your Notion content changes significantly
Analogy: It's like a library organizing all its books, creating an index card for each one, and filing them in a catalog. This takes time, but you only do it once (or occasionally to update).
## Phase 2: Query Time (Every User Question)
This happens every time a user asks a question. It uses the pre-built index.
```plain text
┌─────────────┐
│    User     │  "Who is responsible for marketing?"
│   Question  │
└──────┬──────┘
       │
       ▼
┌─────────────┐
│  Embedding  │  Convert question to a vector
└──────┬──────┘
       │
       ▼
┌─────────────┐
│  Vector DB  │  Find chunks with similar vectors
│   Search    │
└──────┬──────┘
       │
       ▼
┌─────────────┐
│  Top chunks │  "Alice - Marketing Lead...", "Team roster..."
│  retrieved  │
└──────┬──────┘
       │
       ▼
┌─────────────┐
│     LLM     │  "Given this context, answer the question"
└──────┬──────┘
       │
       ▼
┌─────────────┐
│   Answer    │  "Alice is responsible for marketing."
└─────────────┘

```
When does this run?
• Every single time a user asks a question
• Must be fast (seconds, not minutes)
Analogy: A library visitor asks "Do you have books about gardening?" The librarian doesn't re-organize the entire library — they just look up "gardening" in the existing catalog and quickly find matching books.
### Notion Integration
to fetch the data via Notion API
### LLM Integration
To send context+question and get answer. We can try with OpenAI, Claude and other LLM models.

title: Rutvik’s Notes
content:
# Tech Stack Research
## Architecture Diagram
```mermaid
graph TD
    subgraph "Client Side"
        User[User / Developer]
        Frontend["React App (Cloudflare Pages)"]
        User -->|"HTTPS"| Frontend
    end

    subgraph "Serverless Backend (Google Cloud)"
        API["FastAPI (Cloud Run Service)"]
        Scheduler[Cloud Scheduler]
        SyncJob["Python Sync Script (Cloud Run Job)"]
        
        Frontend -->|"1. Query"| API
        Scheduler -->|"Trigger (Hourly)"| SyncJob
    end

    subgraph "Knowledge Base (Memory)"
        Pinecone[(Pinecone Vector DB)]
        Neo4j[(Neo4j Aura Graph DB)]
        
        API <-->|"2. Retrieve Context"| Pinecone
        API <-->|"3. Retrieve Dependencies"| Neo4j
        SyncJob -->|"Upsert Vectors"| Pinecone
        SyncJob -->|"Upsert Nodes"| Neo4j
    end

    subgraph "External Sources"
        Notion[Notion API]
        GitHub[GitHub API]
        GDocs[Google Docs API]
        
        SyncJob <-->|"Read Changes"| Notion
        SyncJob <-->|"Read Changes"| GitHub
        SyncJob <-->|"Read Changes"| GDocs
    end

    subgraph "Private Intelligence (On-Demand)"
        Modal["Modal.com Container"]
        Llama3["Llama 3 (8B Quantized)"]
        
        API -->|"4. Send Prompt + Context"| Modal
        Modal -- Contains --> Llama3
        Modal -->|"5. Return Answer"| API
    end
```
## Stack
### 1. The Frontend (Client)
• Framework: React (built with Vite)
• Hosting: Cloudflare Pages
• Why: It’s free, incredibly fast, and updates automatically whenever you push code to GitHub.
### 2. The Backend (Orchestration)
• Framework: FastAPI (Python)
• Runtime: Google Cloud Run
• Why: It runs your Python code in a container that scales to zero when no one is using it. You get 2 million requests per month for free.
• Logic: LlamaIndex
• Why: Manages the complex "Graph + Vector" retrieval logic.
### 3. The Knowledge Base (Memory)
• Graph Database: Neo4j Aura (Free Tier)
• Why: A managed cloud graph database. The free tier supports up to 50k nodes, which is plenty for your docs.
• Vector Database: Pinecone (Serverless Free Tier)
• Why: The easiest vector DB to use. It handles the "semantic search" part of RAG.
### 4. The Intelligence (The Brain)
• Inference Engine: Modal (or RunPod)
• Model: Llama 3 8B (Quantized)
• Why: You write a simple Python script to wrap the Llama model. Modal deploys it to a GPU only when your FastAPI backend sends a request.
• Privacy: The model runs in your ephemeral container. Data is processed in memory and vanishes when the container spins down. No third-party AI company trains on your data.
## Timeline
### Phase 1: The "Hello World" Skeleton (Weeks 1-2)
Goal: Establish the infrastructure. By the end of this phase, you should have a live URL that returns a raw AI response.
• Infrastructure: Set up the Google Cloud Project, Cloudflare Pages repo, Pinecone (Free), Neo4j Aura (Free), and Modal account.
• Team Data:
• Write a basic modal_app.py script that downloads Llama 3 and serves it via a web endpoint.
• Verify you can send curl requests to Modal and get a text response.
• Team App:
• Initialize the FastAPI repo and deploy a "Health Check" endpoint to Cloud Run.
• Connect FastAPI to the Modal endpoint.
• Deploy a basic "Create React App" (or Vite) to Cloudflare Pages.
### Phase 2: Basic Ingestion & Vector RAG (Weeks 3-4)
Goal: The system can read one source (e.g., Notion) and answer basic questions based on text.
• Team Data (The Ingestion):
• Write the python script to fetch pages from Notion API.
• Implement "Chunking" (splitting text into paragraphs).
• Send chunks to Pinecone.
• Team App (The Retrieval):
• Implement LlamaIndex in the FastAPI backend.
• Connect LlamaIndex to Pinecone.
• Milestone: You can ask "What are the requirements for feature X?" and get a grounded answer.
### Phase 3: The Graph & The Sync (Weeks 5-6)
Goal: The system understands relationships and stays up-to-date automatically.
• Team Data (The Graph):
• Connect Neo4j. Update the ingestion script to create nodes for Documents and CodeFiles.
• Crucial Logic: Implement the "Change Detection" (check timestamps). If a file hasn't changed, skip it.
• Deploy this script as a Cloud Run Job.
• Team App:
• Add a simple "Sync Now" button to the frontend that triggers the Cloud Run Job manually.
### Phase 4: Advanced Logic - "The Inconsistency Detector" (Weeks 7-8)
Goal: The core value proposition. The system compares Requirements vs. Code.
• Team Data:
• Refine the Graph: Ensure you are linking Requirement Docs to specific Code Files (e.g., using file naming conventions or manual tags in Notion).
• Team App:
• Prompt Engineering: Write the specific "Compare" prompt for Llama 3.
• Example: "Context A is the requirement. Context B is the code. List 3 discrepancies."
• Build the backend route that retrieves both Graph connections (Dependencies) and Vector matches (Content) to feed this prompt.
### Phase 5: Visualization & User Experience (Weeks 9-10)
Goal: Make it usable for non-developers.
• Team App (Frontend):
• Implement React Flow (or a similar graph viz library).
• Visual Goal: Show the user a node map of their project. Green nodes = "Consistent", Red nodes = "Conflict Detected".
• Build the "Diff View": Side-by-side comparison of the Requirement text vs. the Code snippet.
• Team Data:
• Test with "Messy" data. intentionally break some code and see if the AI catches it.
### Phase 6: Polish, Automation & Demo (Weeks 11-12)
Goal: Production readiness and final presentation.
• Automation:
• Enable Cloud Scheduler to trigger the Sync Job every hour (or every night).
• Security:
• Add a simple API Key check to your FastAPI backend so the public can't drain your AI credits.
• Documentation:
• Write the README.
• Record a backup demo video (in case live wifi fails).
• Final Deliverable: A live URL, a GitHub repo, and a working demo of "Project Auto-Analysis".
## Monthly Cost Estimation
For a student project, this architecture is extremely cheap:
• Cloudflare Pages: $0
• Google Cloud Run: $0 (Free Tier)
• Neo4j Aura: $0 (Free Tier)
• Pinecone: $0 (Free Tier)
• Modal/RunPod: ~$5 - $10 (You pay ~$0.0005 per second of GPU usage. For dev and demos, this is negligible).
# Research
## Requirements
• Existing products: c3.ai, Atlassian
• TED Talks
• Base Models (LLMs)
## Knowledge Base
• VectorDB Basics
• Embedding, Indexing, Chunking
• Metadata Generation
• HuggingFace + Python Library?

title: Jubaida’s Notes
content:
## AI-Assisted Knowledge & Decision System
### Story
An AI assistant that understands all technical project documents and safely answers questions like a university portal answers “What are my courses and grades?” but for any software system.
Problem
• In real projects, information is scattered: Notion pages, PDFs, code repos, design docs, logs.
• Developers waste time searching or asking teammates things like “Who owns this service?” or “Is this design doc still up to date?”
### Visual demo
Center the demo on a university portal use‑case plus a technical project use‑case:
• Scenario A – Student view (university example):
• User: logged‑in student.
• Questions: “What are my current semester courses?”, “What is my grade in CS595?”, “Who is the professor for CS595 – Computer Science Capstone Course?”.
• System:
• Shows only that student’s current courses and grades.
• Returns “The professor of CS595 is Ahmed Banafa” (from course docs).
• Refuses “What is John’s CS595 grade?” with a clear privacy explanation.
• Scenario B – Engineer view (technical project):
• User: developer on a backend system.
• Questions: “Which service handles payments?”, “Is the logging design up to date with the latest release?”, “Summarize the current system architecture in 5 lines.”
• System:
• Returns answers with citations.
• Shows a side panel: “3 outdated design docs”, “2 modules missing tests.”
Log in as a student → ask 2–3 portal questions → switch to engineer view → ask 2–3 project questions.
### Tech Stack
### Frontend
• React + Tailwind CSS (simple UI, modern look)
• Pages: Upload/Connect, Ask (RAG), Insights Dashboard, Admin/Access
### Backend
• FastAPI (Python) (all APIs in one place)
• Pydantic (schemas/validation)
### Data + Storage
• PostgreSQL (users, roles, documents metadata, permissions, versions, audit logs)
• ChromaDB (vector store for embeddings + chunk metadata)
### Ingestion
• Notion API (primary source)
• File uploads:
• python-docx (DOCX)
• pdfplumber (PDF)
• Markdown parser (basic)
### AI / RAG
• Hosted embeddings API (e.g., OpenAI or similar).
• LLM API (hosted) for Q&A + summaries
• Lightweight RAG orchestration:
• LangChain (or LlamaIndex — choose one)
### Authorization / Privacy (core feature)
• JWT authentication
• RBAC rules in Postgres (Student / Instructor / Team Member / Admin)
• “Refusal layer” in backend before calling LLM (block unauthorized queries)
Deployment
• Local: Docker + Docker Compose (frontend, backend, DB, vector DB).
• Cloud: one of Vercel/Railway/Render/Cloud Run for final presentation ( public URL).
The 12-Week Execution Roadmap
Person A (Backend/RAG), Person B (Data/Auth), Person C (Frontend), Person D (Ingestion/Deployment).
Phase 1: Proposal Demo & Core Stack (Weeks 1–3)
Objective: Week 1 proposal demo + Week 3 professor submission with live prototype.
Week 1 – Proposal Demo
• Finalize & present: Project story, university analogy, tech stack, team roles, 12-week roadmap.
• Person A: FastAPI project with /health endpoint (show live curl).
• Person B: PostgreSQL schema design (users/roles/docs tables).
• Person C: React + Tailwind landing page + login mockup.
• Person D: Docker Compose skeleton (containers start successfully).
• Demo: Show environments running + proposal slides.
Week 2 – Prototype for Week 3 Submission
• Person D: Ingest first Notion page → PostgreSQL + ChromaDB chunks.
• Person A: Stub /query endpoint + hosted embeddings connection.
• Person B: Basic JWT login + Student/Engineer roles.
• Person C: Simple "Ask question" UI showing raw answer.
• Demo prep: Login → "What are my CS595 grades?" → basic response.
Week 3 Milestone: Professor submission - Live demo (login → question → answer), PPT with story/architecture/stack/timeline, 30-sec video.
Phase 2: End-to-End RAG + Week 6 Midterm (Weeks 4–6)
Objective: Full privacy demo + Dockerized stack.
Weeks 4–5:
• Person D: PDF/DOCX parsers + file upload endpoint.
• Person B: ChromaDB retrieval + PostgreSQL metadata sync.
• Person A: Basic RAG pipeline (question → cited answer).
• Person C: Q&A chat interface + citation chips.
Week 6 Milestone: Live demo - Student view (own grades only), privacy refusal ("John's grades? → Policy violation"), engineer view, full stack via docker-compose up.
Phase 3: Advanced Features & Production Polish (Weeks 7–9)
Objective: Interview-ready MVP with cloud deployment.
Weeks 7–8:
• Person A: Insights engine (outdated docs, architecture summary).
• Person D: Notion sync automation + production Docker.
• Person C: Dashboard charts + mobile responsive.
• Person B: Permission analytics + DB optimization.
Week 9 Milestone: Cloud deploy (Vercel frontend + Railway backend) with public URLs.
Phase 4: Final Sprint & Week 12 Presentation (Weeks 10–12)
Objective: Production-ready demo + portfolio pieces.
Week 10:
• Person A: Caching + Swagger API docs.
• Person B: Production data fixtures.
• Person C: Accessibility + component docs.
• Person D: Deployment guide.
Week 11: Full dry run + staging URL published. All practice presentations.
Week 12 Milestone: Production demo - Complete university + project scenarios, cloud link + Docker backup, every member presents.
Demo Flow (Every Phase): Login as student → "My CS595 grade?" (✅) → "John's grade?" (❌ refused) → Switch to engineer → "Payment service?" (cited) → Insights ("3 outdated docs").

title: Emmanuel’s Notes
content:
```markdown
# MVP Tech Stack & Architecture
## AI-Assisted Knowledge & Decision System for Technical Projects

### High-Level Architecture

```
┌─────────────────┐
│   Data Sources  │
│  (Notion, Docs, │
│      Code)      │
└────────┬────────┘
         │
         ▼
┌─────────────────────────────────────┐
│      Ingestion Layer                │
│  (ETL Pipeline - Python)            │
└────────┬─────────────────────────────┘
         │
         ├──────────────────┬──────────────────┐
         ▼                  ▼                  ▼
┌──────────────┐  ┌──────────────┐  ┌──────────────┐
│  Metadata    │  │  Embeddings  │  │  Version     │
│  Store       │  │  Vector DB   │  │  Tracking    │
│  (PostgreSQL)│  │  (Chroma/    │  │  (PostgreSQL)│
│              │  │   Qdrant)    │  │              │
└──────────────┘  └──────────────┘  └──────────────┘
         │                  │                  │
         └──────────────────┴──────────────────┘
                            │
                            ▼
                  ┌─────────────────┐
                  │   RAG Pipeline  │
                  │   (LangChain/   │
                  │   LlamaIndex)   │
                  └────────┬────────┘
                           │
         ┌─────────────────┼─────────────────┐
         ▼                 ▼                 ▼
┌──────────────┐  ┌──────────────┐  ┌──────────────┐
│   Q&A API    │  │  Insights    │  │  Analytics   │
│   (FastAPI)  │  │  Generator   │  │  Dashboard   │
└──────────────┘  └──────────────┘  └──────────────┘
```

### Recommended Tech Stack for MVP

#### 1. **Data Ingestion & Processing**
- **Language**: Python 3.11+
- **ETL Framework**: 
  - `langchain` / `llama-index` for document processing
  - `pandas` for data manipulation
  - `notion-sdk-py` for Notion API integration
- **Document Parsers**:
  - `pypandoc` / `python-docx` for Word docs
  - `markdown` for Markdown files
  - `tree-sitter` or `pygments` for code parsing

#### 2. **Storage Layer**
- **Metadata & Version Tracking**: PostgreSQL 15+
  - Stores document metadata, relationships, versions
  - Use `pgvector` extension for hybrid search (optional)
- **Vector Database**: 
  - **Option A (MVP)**: ChromaDB (embedded, no separate server)
  - **Option B (Scalable)**: Qdrant (better performance, separate service)
  - **Option C (Cloud)**: Pinecone (managed, pay-per-use)

#### 3. **RAG Pipeline**
- **Framework**: 
  - `langchain` (more flexible, active community)
  - OR `llama-index` (simpler API, good for RAG)
- **Embeddings Model**:
  - `sentence-transformers` with `all-MiniLM-L6-v2` (lightweight, fast)
  - OR `text-embedding-3-small` from OpenAI (better quality, API-based)
- **Chunking Strategy**:
  - Semantic chunking with `langchain.text_splitter.RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter`
  - Overlap: 200 chars, chunk size: 1000-1500 tokens

#### 4. **LLM & AI**
- **LLM Provider** (for MVP):
  - **OpenAI GPT-4o-mini** (cost-effective, high quality)
  - OR **Anthropic Claude 3 Haiku** (good for technical content)
  - OR **OpenRouter** (access to multiple models)
- **Local Option** (if needed): `ollama` with `llama3` or `mistral`

#### 5. **API Layer**
- **Framework**: FastAPI (async, auto-docs, type hints)
- **Authentication**: JWT tokens (simple for MVP)
- **API Gateway**: None needed for MVP (add nginx later if needed)

#### 6. **Frontend (Optional for MVP)**
- **Option A**: Simple Streamlit app (fastest MVP)
- **Option B**: Next.js + React (if you need a proper web app)
- **Option C**: Python CLI (if no UI needed initially)

#### 7. **Orchestration & Deployment**
- **Containerization**: Docker + Docker Compose
- **Orchestration**: 
  - Local: Docker Compose
  - Cloud: AWS ECS, Google Cloud Run, or Railway
- **Task Queue** (if async processing needed):
  - `celery` + Redis (for background jobs)
  - OR `dramatiq` (simpler alternative)
  - OR SQS

#### 8. **Monitoring & Logging**
- **Logging**: Python `logging` + structured logs (JSON)
- **Monitoring**: 
  - Basic: Print logs to stdout (captured by Docker)
  - Better: Sentry (error tracking) or DataDog (full monitoring)

### MVP Architecture Decisions

#### Phase 1: Core MVP (Weeks 1-2)
1. **Ingestion**: Notion API → Parse → Store metadata in PostgreSQL
2. **Embeddings**: Generate embeddings → Store in ChromaDB (embedded)
3. **RAG**: Simple Q&A endpoint with source citation
4. **API**: FastAPI with one endpoint: `/query`

#### Phase 2: Enhanced MVP (Weeks 3-4)
1. Add Markdown/Word doc ingestion
2. Add version tracking
3. Add basic insights (outdated docs detection)
4. Add simple frontend (Streamlit)

#### Phase 3: Production-Ready (Weeks 5-6)
1. Add code repository ingestion
2. Advanced insights (inconsistency detection)
3. Analytics dashboard
4. Proper authentication & deployment

### File Structure Recommendation

```
final_project/
├── ingestion/
│   ├── notion_ingestion.py
│   ├── document_parser.py
│   └── code_parser.py
├── storage/
│   ├── metadata_db.py
│   ├── vector_store.py
│   └── version_tracker.py
├── rag/
│   ├── chunking.py
│   ├── retrieval.py
│   └── generation.py
├── api/
│   ├── main.py (FastAPI app)
│   ├── routes/
│   │   ├── query.py
│   │   └── insights.py
│   └── models.py
├── insights/
│   ├── inconsistency_detector.py
│   └── outdated_doc_detector.py
├── docker-compose.yml
├── requirements.txt
└── README.md
```

### Key Dependencies (requirements.txt)

```txt
# Core

uvicorn[standard]==0.24.0
pydantic==2.5.0

# Database
psycopg2-binary==2.9.9

# Vector DB

# OR qdrant-client==1.7.0

langchain-openai==0.0.2
langchain-community==0.0.10

python-docx==1.1.0
markdown==3.5.1

# Utilities

```

### Cost Estimates (MVP)

- **OpenAI API**: ~$10-50/month (depending on usage)
- **PostgreSQL**: Free (local) or $5-15/month (managed)
- **Vector DB**: Free (ChromaDB embedded) or $0-20/month (Qdrant/Pinecone)
- **Hosting**: $0-20/month (Railway/Render free tier or small instance)
- **Total MVP**: ~$15-85/month

### Next Steps

1. Set up project structure
2. Create Docker Compose with PostgreSQL + ChromaDB
3. Implement Notion ingestion
4. Build basic RAG pipeline
5. Create FastAPI endpoint
6. Test end-to-end flow
